coredata.dc:title: Smart nanomaterials as the foundation of a combination approach for efficient cancer theranostics
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, S.
Hajra, S.
Kaushik, A.
Rubahn, H.G.
Mishra, Y.K.
Kim, H.J.
coredata.dc:description: Despite numerous FDA-approved treatment and diagnostic approaches, cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide due to delayed diagnostics and the limited efficacy of treatments. The life quality of cancer patients is currently improving due to the development of nanotechnology-supported cancer diagnostics, detection, and nanomedicine for targeted drug delivery approaches investigated for cancer therapy. Considering these aspects, this comprehensive review presents the potential of smart nanomaterials (SMs) including carbon nanodots, metal-phenolic networks (MNPs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (QDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal/metal oxide nanoparticles, and silica nanoparticles, as well as polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, amino acids, proteins, natural polysaccharides, and solid lipid nanoparticles, to manage cancer efficiently in a personalized manner. The outcomes of studies published in the last five years are critically analyzed in this review along with challenges and prospects in terms of a wide range of imaging technologies and therapeutic approaches, such as radiation responsive theranostics, magnetic responsive theranostics, drug delivery, photothermal/photodynamic therapy, and gene therapy. We believe that a novel multidisciplinary and combinational approach can be developed for early-stage cancer diagnostics and treat the diseases with efficacy using nano-based sensors, imaging agents, and pharmacologically relevant cargos. To promote research from the laboratory to clinical application, this review discusses viewpoints based on the tremendous potential of new and developing nanomaterials as a potential platform for cancer theranostics.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite numerous FDA-approved treatment and diagnostic approaches, cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide due to delayed diagnostics and the limited efficacy of treatments. The life quality of cancer patients is currently improving due to the development of nanotechnology-supported cancer diagnostics, detection, and nanomedicine for targeted drug delivery approaches investigated for cancer therapy. Considering these aspects, this comprehensive review presents the potential of smart nanomaterials (SMs) including carbon nanodots, metal-phenolic networks (MNPs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (QDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal/metal oxide nanoparticles, and silica nanoparticles, as well as polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, amino acids, proteins, natural polysaccharides, and solid lipid nanoparticles, to manage cancer efficiently in a personalized manner. The outcomes of studies published in the last five years are critically analyzed in this review along with challenges and prospects in terms of a wide range of imaging technologies and therapeutic approaches, such as radiation responsive theranostics, magnetic responsive theranostics, drug delivery, photothermal/photodynamic therapy, and gene therapy. We believe that a novel multidisciplinary and combinational approach can be developed for early-stage cancer diagnostics and treat the diseases with efficacy using nano-based sensors, imaging agents, and pharmacologically relevant cargos. To promote research from the laboratory to clinical application, this review discusses viewpoints based on the tremendous potential of new and developing nanomaterials as a potential platform for cancer theranostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Theranostics is a revolutionary technique with enormous clinical potential that incorporates diagnostics and medications in a more customized manner to improve health and safety [1]. Multimodal imaging-guided treatment of cancer can be achieved by smart nanomaterials (SMs), which provide a platform for cancer therapy [2]. In early 1990, Prof Tagaki introduced the concept of SMs and suggested that these materials can automatically manifest their functioning as per environmental change [3]. After that, new terms such as “stimuli-responsive materials” or “smart materials” have attracted great interest with innovations in nanotechnology. SMs are a class of materials with wide functionalities and can be tailored by reactions to different stimuli. These functionalities such as nanosized particles, higher surface area, excellent absorption, and magnetic and optical properties offer the flexibility to be applied in various biomedical applications. They can be simulated or influenced by some external stimuli, such as magnetic fields, electrical fields, and thermally responsive changes [4]. The assessment of the effectiveness of therapy, and early cancer recognition, can be performed by SM-based cancer therapies, as well as imaging probes with suitable physicochemical properties [5]. Magnetic nanoparticles, quantum dots (QDs), and nanosilica particles act as efficient cancer theranostic agents. Numerous nanomaterial-based therapies, imaging agents, and methods are permitted for medical use, while several discoveries are at the beginning stages of imaging and therapy [6].

Cancer is a leading cause of death and morbidity after cardiovascular diseases globally [7]. Cancer can occur rapidly and affects approximately half of men and one-third of women in the United States [8]. In the United States in 2021, an estimated 1.9 million cancer cases were diagnosed, with 608,570 cancer deaths [9]. The number of deaths from cancer is estimated to reach 30 million by 2030 as predicted by the Global Cancer Observatory [10]. The SARS-CoV-2 pandemic has also had a significant impact on cancer care pathways [11] as this pandemic is directly linked to treatment delays and missed opportunities in terms of anticancer therapy and quality of life for example chemotherapy, radiation, and surgeries are being postponed, and there is restricted access to supportive care [12,13]. Due to early diagnosis and intervention, millions of cancer patients are now living longer lives [14]. Recent advancements in nanotechnology such as SMs have opened new possibilities [15,16]. These technologies have the potential to be game-changers in cancer prevention, detection, diagnosis, imaging, and therapy [17]. Nanoparticles can target and image tumors, detect pathophysiological abnormalities in tumor cells, deliver therapeutic genes or medications, respond to external triggers to control drug release, and identify remnant tumor cells, among other things [18,19]. These characteristics can be tweaked to make them suitable for specific biomedical use in cancer theranostics [20]. There is now a high demand for anticancer theranostics that detect and target tumor cells directly and selectively while posing minimal harm.

This review paper summarizes the most recent advances in the development of SMs, with a focus on cancer theranostic applications. The unique characteristics of various organic and inorganic nanomaterials can act as novel routes for cancer diagnosis and therapy. Nanoparticles reduce the drug effect and improve the efficiency of treatment by accumulating at the tumor site through permeability and retention. In this review most recent achievements for cancer therapies using nanomaterials such as inorganic nanomaterials and organic materials with the synthesis route, therapeutic drug delivery and tumor imaging are highlighted. Along with the most recent developments in cancer theranostics research employing SMs, the opinions on how the research has been moved from the lab to clinical applications is also discussed. First, the paper discusses the importance of nanotechnology in cancer therapy and the various types of nanomaterial-based cancer therapy. In summary, several nanomaterials such as inorganic nanomaterials including metal-phenolic networks (MNPs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dot (QDs), carbon nanomaterials, metal/metal oxide nanoparticles, and silica nanoparticles, as well as organic materials such as polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, amino acids, natural polysaccharides, proteins or peptides, and solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) are discussed in detail. Table 1 presents various types of inorganic and organic SMs as cancer theranostics. Finally, the current challenges and future prospects for developing nanomaterials as cancer theranostic nanoplatforms are discussed. The objective of this review is to provide readers with a concise overview of the recent development of nanotechnology for cancer theranostics and prospective future advancements and up-to-date information about SM-related applications for cancer theranostics.

Cancer is a complex and disorganized disease with a multiphase carcinogenic process involving numerous physiological treatments such as detection and apoptotic cell death. Early detection of cancer is very crucial to initiate better and more effective treatment [21]. The traditional treatments for cancer are chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or excision which are quite expensive and take long amounts of time [22]. Nanotechnology has the potential to revolutionize cancer treatment, making it less costly and easier [23]. Three key aspects are necessary for cancer therapy which are single or dual mode screening, retargeting with one or more ligands, and the use of a wide assortment of other medicines [24]. Nanotechnology provides ample of possibilities for fine-tuning certain aspects that are impossible to arrange in other therapies [25]. As a result, nanotechnology seems to have a promising and prolonged future for the early diagnosis of cancer and therapy [26]. The rise in nanotechnology has been effective in developing nanomedicines, such as nano vectors and nanoparticulate drug delivery for cancer therapeutics [27]. Clinicians have successfully achieved certain ways of treating cancer using nanoparticles, as they deliver the drug to the targeted organ, can differentiate between normal and tumor cells, and have the potential to target the nucleus of the cell [28,29].

Nanomaterials are referred to as nanosized particles, with sizes ranging from 1 to 100 nm and feature superior properties compared to the same material in bulk [30]. They can be distinguished as zero, one, two, or three-dimensional considering the overall shape of the nanoparticles. The core (central part), surface layer, and shell layer are basic layers in nanoparticles [31]. The wide range of properties, such as large surface area, high electrical conductivity, excellent optical properties, and magnetic, fluorescence nature makes nanoparticles applicable in several areas such as carriers for drug therapy, and nanomedicines [32,33]. Controlled drug release at the disease site has been achieved by nanoparticle-based drug delivery units [34]. Biocompatibility and enhanced permeability to cross vascular or epithelial barriers are some of the important features of nanomaterials [35]. The large surface area of nanoparticles acts as an important feature of drug delivery, as they functionalize quite well with various drugs. Nanoparticles can improve the circulation period of drugs by controlled release, hence reducing systemic toxicity [36]. Early-stage recognition of cancer cells can be detected by various fluorescent nanoparticles and QDs [37].

Theranostics pave the way toward the precise diagnosis and treatment of cancer by merging therapeutic features and functionality into a unified system. This approach is more effective in boosting detection ability and therapeutic efficiency highlighting the role of nanotechnology in the medical sciences [38]. Furthermore, nano theranostic agents are capable of on-demand activity, suggesting promising prospect [39]. For example, radiation, light, magnetic fields, and sound waves, have solidified their positions as the next generation of anticancer candidates. There have been many reports of these stimuli-responsive theranostic nanomaterials being used to treat cancer, but few have made it to medical studies, much less medical practice [40].

The rapid advancement of nanotechnology has allowed the development of a variety of engineered nanocarrier systems, including liposomal, dendrimer, polymeric, organic, and inorganic nanoparticles, metal oxides, QDs, and MOFs [41]. Such nanoparticles have shown that they can deliver medicinal substances and other desired contents to specific regions with near-perfect accuracy [42]. They have also been used as depots and carriers for nanomaterials, including hydrophobic substances, enhancing pharmacokinetics, increasing their durability and preservation in the bloodstream, and reducing unwanted side effects by allowing for tissue-specific delivery [43,44]. Fig. 1 provides an overview of this review elucidating nanoparticles, features of nanoparticles, and bio-targeted cancer theranostic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of cancer theranostics using smart nanomaterials

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer treatment uses intense dosages of radiation to treat cancer malignancies. X-rays use minimal doses of radiation to see the inside of the body. This type of therapy is known as radiation therapy (RT) [45]. Although RT is most commonly used to treat squamous cell carcinoma, it can also treat various cancers since it immediately damages nuclear DNA, which inhibits cell growth [46]. Following surgical resection, RT has documented long-term survival. Radiation can indirectly damage mitochondrial DNA and other cellular constituents by reactive oxygen species (ROS). Radiation resistance and the systemic adverse effects of chronic high-dose X-ray exposure are also important disadvantages of RT [47]. Metallic nanoparticles (NPs) with high photoelectric absorbance can enhance RT sensitivity while also functioning as radiation dose enhancers [48]. Clement et al. discussed that nanomaterials with appropriate engineering can improve cancer radiation by boosting tumor targeting and reducing side effects [49]. Molecular targeting, drug/gene delivery, and adaptive responses to induce drug release are all therapeutically important features of the nanomaterial platform [50,51].

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an important clinical imaging technology that delivers noninvasive, real-time positional accuracy with soft tissue contrast without ionizing radiation or radiotracers [52]. Under a large external magnetic field (MF), these magnetic moment orientations of protons inside a material cause MRI to work [53]. When employing MRI, the magnetic moments of the samples are coupled with those of the surrounding proton, resulting in spin depetris and a decrease in proton recreational time [54]. Multimode imaging on a single device, such as T1–T2 dual-weighted MRI, has recently gained popularity [55]. New T1-W and T2-W contrast agents are constantly being developed to increase the accuracy of MR signals versus their surroundings. T1W contrast NPs containing iron, gadolinium, and manganese are particularly useful for obtaining structural data, T2W contrast is superior for diagnosing swelling and infection, iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) are commonly used [56]. IONPs have recently been used as nanocarriers for a variety of bioactive compounds, some of which have even been used in the clinic [57]. Magnetic NPs have been used to deliver several well-known chemotherapeutics to cancer tissue [58].

This treatment involves infecting tumor cells with plasmid DNA (pDNA) containing a gene that causes tumor genetic defects producing a protein that causes the cell to die [59]. Cell death is induced by a similar approach to apoptosis known as apoptosis-induced gene therapy or protein toxicity known as toxin-induced gene therapy. The occurrence of pDNA in cancer cells has challenged the implementation of this therapy [60]. The most recent gene vector deployment involves the use of synthetic polymer or inorganic NPs with physiochemical and biological properties (structure, dimensions, charge density, moisture engagement, and decomposition or degradation percentage) that allow the pDNA to be delivered into cancerous cells. Furthermore, nanoparticles with specific molecules can be functionalized to recognize biomarkers on the surface of cancerous cells [16]. The nanoparticle binds to the tumor cell, causing selective endocytosis and minimizing the cytotoxicity to healthy tissue. Currently, there are no approved clinical procedures for the use of NPs in death-induced gene therapy [61]. Although there are still several obstacles to overcome in developing the ideal transfection vector, nanoparticles are a viable option.

Due to the exceptional properties of photosensitizers such as negligible invasiveness and high-quality imaging, light-mediated therapeutics have recently been acknowledged as an extremely competitive advantage over many traditional therapies [54]. The properties of light-responsive theranostic precisely control the initiation of therapeutic materials and image analysis probes in vitro and in vivo [62]. Traditional light-mediated theranostic strategies rely on short-wavelength light (e.g., UV or visible light), which has several drawbacks, including a low tissue absorption coefficient, inescapable optical absorption, and possible phototoxicity to normal tissue [63]. As a result, a near-infrared (NIR) light-mediated approach based on long-wavelength light (700–1000 nm) treatment with deep tissue penetration, minimum photo-damage, and low autofluorescence in living systems has been established [64,65]. When the photosensitizer (PS) molecule is exposed to the light directly, it absorbs energy from light irradiates in a specific wavelength range and then enters a highly unstable state [66]. This highly unstable state then transfers energy to nearby oxygen molecules, producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) that can harm nearby biomolecules, convert absorbed energy into heat, increase the local temperature, or release energy by emitting photons with lower power, eventually causing the activated molecules to turn into toxic substances [67,68]. According to the diverse functional mechanisms of photosensitive materials, two distinct types of light-triggered therapy, photodynamic therapy (PDT), and photothermal therapy (PTT) are used by nanomaterials.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) uses lasers, either a constant wave or cyclic to damage carcinoma with electromagnetic waves (e.g., visible or near-infrared), causing a massive temperature increase in which incident light energy is transformed into heat, leading to apoptosis via multiple pathways [69,70]. To access the nuclei of solid tumors, PTT converts photon energy into significant heat that remains distributed in the neighboring tissues. PTT is independent of oxygen concentration within tumors. Tumors are more sensitive to hyperthermia than normal tissues because of the disorganized and irregular vasculature of the tumor tissue, which results in a lower pH and oxygen content inside the tumor environment. Tumor cells undergo mitochondrial swelling, protein denaturation, and membrane rupture at temperatures of 40 °C–45 °C, whereas normal tissues exposed to these temperatures for an hour do not experience any major damage [71,72]. PSs with relatively short pulse widths compared to the normal thermoelectric recreational time of body tissue can cause severe necrosis by generating intense thermoelectric containment inside the target cell. By perfectly controlling laser specifications and concentrating on the target spot, PSs can be sensitized and the intended imaging or therapeutic impact can be implemented precisely at the lesion, without affecting nearby cells or tissues [73]. Many nanomaterials have the potential to respond to near-infrared (NIR) and convert light energy into heat [74,75]. For theranostics, gold NPs [76], carbon NPs, 2D NPs [77], semiconductors [78], and other inorganic NPs [79] have all been developed.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is rapidly growing as a therapeutic approach, with organic and inorganic PSs serving as their primary instruments [80]. When exposed to laser light, PSs transfer the stored energy to nearby oxygen molecules, resulting in the generation of free radicals or ROS that cause apoptotic cell death and the destruction of diseased tissue [81,82]. PTT generates hyperthermia with a greater laser power intensity, whereas PDT causes cell death with a much lower laser power frequency [83]. When photosensitizers are activated by certain wavelengths of light, energy transfer cascades are set off, producing cytotoxic ROS that can cause necrosis and apoptosis in nearby tissues. Utilizing this functional mechanism in tumor therapy hence offers a safe and manageable method of targeting tumors for eradication with low systemic toxicity and side effects on vital cells [84]. Three conditions must be met for photodynamic treatment to function: light, photosensitizers, and oxygen. Therefore, a sufficient oxygen concentration is a necessary component for maximum effectiveness. The effectiveness of photodynamic therapy greatly depends on the subcellular location of the PSs since ROS developed based on photodynamic mechanisms has an uncommonly short half-life and a narrow range of use. Some advantages of PDT include its minimal invasion, reduced harm to the hemopoietic and immunological systems, and decreased cytotoxicity, all of which contribute to a safer therapy option for patients [85]. A perfect PS would have low dark toxicity, a large quantum yield of triplet state production, and a significant triplet lifespan for interacting with ground state oxygen to generate ROS [86]. The next sections will discuss various nanomaterials that can react to NIR and convert light energy into heat [87].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different types of smart nanomaterials for cancer theranostics
ce:section.ce:para: Organic and inorganic nanoscale compounds employed in cancer treatments can be extensively differentiated [88]. Polymeric NPs, liposomes, protein-based nanomaterials, and solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) are only a few examples of organic materials, metal-phenolic networks (MNPs), Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (QDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal/metal oxide nanoparticles, and silica nanoparticles are examples of inorganic nanocarriers.

Inorganic nanomaterials have been employed for cancer therapy due to qualities such as ease of manufacturing and a large surface area for the conjugation of various compounds [89]. Inorganic nanoparticles also have several desirable features that make them suitable for use as theranostic agents, such as imaging, PTT, PDT, and so on [5,90,91]. Regarding cancer therapy, the following section of this review focuses on inorganic smart nanomaterials.

Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) can be employed in photoacoustic (PA) imaging, plasmonic photothermal treatment (PPTT), and tunable surface plasmon resonances (SPRs) due to their unique optical features, biocompatibility, limited production, and customizable surface area [92]. Continuous-wave (CW) lasers are commonly employed in PPTT because of their compact size and inexpensive cost. If pulsed-wave (PW) lasers could be utilized to damage tissue, integrative theranostic applications such as PA-guided PPTT could be performed using the same laser system and gold nanorods (AuNRs). As a critical stage in the evolution of AuNRs for lung cancer treatment, Knights et al. studied the effect of AuNR size on PA responsiveness, PW-PPTT effectiveness, and PA imaging in a tissue-mimicking phantom. Under PW illumination at a laser wavelength below the maximum permissible skin exposure, all AuNRs demonstrated toxicity, with the smallest AuNRs exhibiting up to 80% cell death, demonstrating the feasibility of PW-PPTT [93]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) also have excellent optical properties, such as high absorbance values, SPR, and UV–vis absorption, and have been effectively used to develop a variety of biological recognition platforms that are easy to use and relatively inexpensive [94]. AgNPs were grown on top of tannic acid (TA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by Hai et al. who developed a multifunctional AgNP@GQDs nanocomposite. The nanocomposite degrades in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), with Ag0 oxidized to Ag+ and oxidized TA and GQDs released. The nanocomposites function efficiently in cancer cell recognition and therapies by combining the H2O2-responsive decrease of AgNPs@GQDs with high H2O2 levels in cancerous cells, in which the symbiotic anticancer effect of Ag+ and oxidized TA influence the effectiveness of cell death, and the conquered GQDs are used to supervise the therapeutic efficacy by cell imaging [95].

Carbon nanodots (CDs) are carbonaceous nanomaterials that are typically made up of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen. CDs are gaining traction as prospective nanoplatforms for a range of uses, including drug delivery, cell imaging, and sensors [96,97]. In the realm of theranostic nanomedicine, CDs have been demonstrated to exhibit a variety of appealing features, including controlled photoluminescence (PL), light-induced photothermal transformation, excellent hydrophilicity, excellent biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity [98]. Researchers working on lengthy light-emitting CD-based nanoplatforms have become interested in multicolor fluorescence, which ranges from blue to near-infrared (NIR), as a novel powerful fluorescence imaging (FLI) technique that is useful in cancer diagnostics [99]. Due to their ability to transform light energy into local temperature increases, CDs have also proven to be excellent photothermal agents, making them beneficial in cancer PTT. Scialabba et al. discovered and reported a simple and highly controlled layer-by-layer production of biotin-decorated CDs. CDs-PEG-BT have a carbon-containing core that has been passivated with biotin-terminated PEG2000 strands that act as targeted therapeutic groups to detect cancerous cells. CDs-PEG-BT was designed to rapidly absorb a significant proportion of anticancer medications, such as irinotecan (16–28%), and to function as NIR-activated nano heaters, resulting in additional hyperthermia and major drug delivery inside tumors, ultimately leading to tumor death [100] that is completely unexpected and productive. Because of their effective characteristics in cancer PTT, CDs have emerged as luminescent nanomaterials [101].

Fig. 2 presents how Tang et al. investigated albumin-integrated platinum nanodots (Pt-NDs) in photoinduced cancer theranostics. All of these nanodots can generate photoacoustic and X-ray transmissions while also producing spectacular photothermal transformations in the presence of NIR light via nonradiative relaxation, resulting in improved micron-sized cell imaging and morphological CT imaging, as well as distinct in vivo heat exhaustion at tumors [102]. The albumin-directed growth kinetics facilitate the growth of tetravalent Pt ions into ultrasmall nanodots leading to the development of monodisperse 6.7 nm Pt-NDs with albumin molecules as the core. Pt-NDs have a surface plasmon resonance at 225 nm, which results in increased NIR absorption, excellent photobleaching resistance, and unique photoacoustic and X-ray signals. The nanomaterial displays striking photothermal effects by non-radiative relaxation in the presence of NIR light. Pt-NDs in particular have a superior in vivo excretory capability and favor tumor proliferation. Therefore, these NDs enable superior in vivo microscopic photoacoustics and spatially anatomic CT imaging with improved contrast in addition to effective tumor removal induced by heat. The findings shed light on the processing of high-performance noble metal nanostructures with therapeutic properties for efficient cancer theranostics.

Metal-phenolic networks (MPNs) have drawn increasing attention to the construction of versatile platforms in recent years [103], because of their simple manufacturing techniques, remarkable physicochemical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and prospective theranostic applications [104]. Targeted delivery of PS to cancer cells remains a major issue for reducing side effects. Thus, relying on monodispersed hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether (HMME) doped CaCO3 templates and using fluorescent radiometric sensing and the regional distribution of ROS, Wei et al. designed an HMME-doped polyethylene glycol (PEG) -MPN capsule, that is pH sensitive and observed that enclosed PSs are spread from lysosomes to the cytoplasm. ROS created by PSs caused cancer cells to die under 638 nm laser irradiation, whereas normal cells survived. As a result, MPN@HMME might be employed as a new approach for cancer-targeted PDT, while PEG-MPN capsules could be used as basic drug carriers [105]. Because of their advantageous physical, chemical, medical and biological properties, such as a wide range of raw resources, low cytotoxicity, selective permeability, excellent dimensional stability, biodegradability, and external stimuli-responsive features, MPNs, particularly those with a simple capsule structure, have attracted the interest of drug carriers. Focusing on these advantages, Dai et al. developed a ROS-promoting combination drug delivery system (DOX@Pt prodrug FeIII nanomaterials, DPPF NPs) oriented on a self-assembly process based on FeIII-MPNs for the co-delivery of platinum prodrugs and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) [98]. Zhang et al. synthesized a biocompatible cRGD/Pt + DOX@GFNPs (RPDG) nano-formulation that disrupted redox equilibrium in glioblastoma cells, improving apoptosis and ferroptosis in tandem. MRI and in vivo findings following pharmacological interventions by tail vein injection suggest that RPDGs concentrate in the tumor locations of mice. The introduction of NIR accelerated and boosted the combination of the therapeutic benefits of apoptosis and ferroptosis. By reducing Pt (IV) to Pt (III), the Fenton reaction generated by GFNPs resulted in enhanced glutathione consumption (II). Research indicates that RPDGs directly suppress cancers and boost the immune system compared to conventional therapeutic methods. Furthermore, RPDGs have excellent MRI and tracer properties. They induce ferroptosis by disrupting intracellular redox homeostasis, which improves the efficacy of the widely used broad-spectrum anticancer drugs used to treat glioma. Hence, this work paves the way for a novel approach for targeting high amounts of H2O2 and glutathione in glioblastoma cells to treat glioma [106] (Fig. 3 ).

Drug delivery has been accomplished using MOFs or coordination polymers [107]; however, limited research has explored how particle size affects tumor targeting and other in vitro characteristics [108]. This problem is most likely caused by two issues: (1) the lack of a biocompatible approach for correctly managing the size of drug-loaded MOFs, and (2) the inability to track particles in vivo using robust and simple radiolabeling technology [109,110]. Duan et al. developed a 64Cu-labeled technique without the need for chelators, and the drug-loaded MOFs were found to be taken up by cancer cells in a size-dependent manner [111]. Using positron emission tomography imaging, which was utilized to objectively analyze MOF in living animals, the in vivo biodistribution of drug-loaded MOF was investigated, and it was discovered that 60 nm MOF had significantly longer blood circulation and significantly greater tumor accumulation than 130 nm MOF. 2D MOF nanosheets have great physicochemical characteristics and distinctive configurations that make them relevant in biomedical domains. Due to their capacity for light-triggered ROS production and effective NIR light absorption, several 2D MOF nanosheets have recently been employed as phototherapy agents. For instance, Zhu et al. have reported using Zn-TCPP nanosheets as extremely effective photosensitizers for cancer PDT [112]. In comparison to their nanoparticle counterparts, these 2D MOF nanosheets produced more light-triggered singlet oxygen and had a larger drug loading capacity, making them suitable for PDT and chemotherapy. These 2D MOF nanosheets demonstrated effective biodegradation, quick renal clearance, and a substantial synergistic anticancer impact in vivo.

Ti-based composite nanomaterials have demonstrated great potential as cancer theranostic agents in recent decades [113]. MXene is a new category of two-dimensional (2D) early transition metal carbides that include titanium carbide (Ti3C2) [114]. Fig. 4 highlights the surface properties of the MXene (Ti3C2–IONPs) with in situ Fe3O4 nanocrystals on the edge of Ti3C2 MXenes. Such magnetized Ti3C2–IONP MXene compounds have a high T2 relaxivity of 394.2 Mm/s and can be utilized for PTT guidance in contrast-enhanced MR imaging of tumors. These magnetic MXenes bear a high photothermal conversion effectiveness of 48.6%, ensuring efficient photothermal-mediated apoptosis and elimination of tumor tissues, as observed in in-vitro and in-vivo studies. Hence, Ti3C2 MXene is an outstanding 2D PTT material that can reduce tumors [115]. Recently, a magnetic titanium carbide MXene nanocomposite was developed for high-throughput cancer therapeutics [116]. Because of its colocalized surface plasmon resonances, all of these studies show that Ti-based composites can be effective photothermal and imaging agents [116]. As PTT agents, titanium dioxide (TiO2) NPs were used by Behnam et al. Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-TiO2 NPs (PEGylated TiO2 NPs) were synthesized to increase water dispersibility and biocompatibility, and PEGylated TiO2 NPs were exceptionally effective in destroying solid tumors [117]. The melanoma tumor in vivo model was used for the investigation. After injecting the TiO2-PEG NPs into the tumor, the tumor sites were exposed to an 808 CW laser beam to evaluate the PTT effects of the NPs. The results indicated that localized NPs significantly increased necrosis because of TiO2-PEG's strong photoabsorption at 808 nm and the deep penetration of the NIR beam into the body. The results of the animal investigations showed that in the TiO2+ laser group, the tumor size also decreased in addition to the tumor growth ceasing. 90% of the tissue in the TiO2+ laser group was necrotic, as revealed by the histological investigation, demonstrating the TiO2-PEG NP high efficacy for the PTT method. This TiO2-PEG NP application for in vivo trials has been described, and it may hold promise as a biocompatible agent for cancer therapeutic methods.

Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) are gaining popularity in cancer diagnosis and treatment due to their tunable nanostructures, simple fabrication method, and stable chemical modification [118,119]. However, their biodegradability is still a challenge. Therefore, Yang et al. designed a biodegradable multifaceted drug carrier for cancer theranostics, bMSN-ss-GABA. The bovine serum albumin-based Gd/Au complex (BSA-Gd/Au) was enclosed on the edge of the bMSN through disulfide linkage, functioning as a contrast material for MR and fluorescence imaging, and then folate (FA) was modified as a specifically focused ligand on the nanocarriers, whose receptor is highly expressed in keratin forming KB human oral epidermoid carcinoma cell lines. DOX@bMSN-ss-GABA was discovered to be redox-responsive, prompting fast drug release in a tumor microenvironment with high glutathione levels [120]. Fig. 5 shows a multifunctional platform for cancer theranostics. Lu et al. processed mesoporous carbon nanospheres (Meso-CNs), a type of carbon nanotube utilized for photothermal and photoacoustic imaging owing to their broad and strong absorption in the UV–Vis NIR region (300–1400 nm). Good efficiency (30.5 wt%) was achieved when DOX was injected into unusual mesoporous CNs. Meso-CN drug release is particularly responsive to both pH and NIR light activation. Under NIR laser treatment, in vivo chemo-photothermal combination therapy shows a notable inhibitory effect on cancer cell growth [121]. Concluding from various studies, drug encapsulation potential, outstanding photothermal and photoacoustic fabrication, and obvious chemo-photothermal therapeutic impact make meso-CNs effective for cancer theranostics. The porous structure additionally acts as a drug carrier, with pH and external light controlling drug release [122].

The most researched material class for biomedical applications, such as magnetic separation of biomolecules and cells, in vivo tumor diagnostics, and stem cell trafficking, is that of magnetic nanoparticles made of iron-based metal oxides [123,124]. The magnetism value of magnetic nanoparticles is dependent on both their composition and crystallinity [125]. Superparamagnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) magnetic nanoparticles were employed by Semkina et al. as an MRI contrast agent. Using a superparamagnetic Fe3O4 core with high T2 relaxivity, a bovine serum albumin (BSA) shell that binds the anticancer drug doxorubicin (Dox), and a poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) corona that increases stability and biocompatibility, the team created new pH-responsive theranostic core-shell-corona nanoparticles [126]. Due to their potential uses in fields such as spintronics and nano theranostics, superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) have received much attention over the past few decades [127]. Monodispersed SPIONs coated with PEG and PEI polymers and modified with folic acid were synthesized by Huang et al. (FA). As an anticancer drug example, doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded within FA-SPIONs (DOX@FA-SPIONs). In low pH PBS, the DOX release rate of DOX@FA-SPIONs was significantly higher. For advanced cancer theranostics, FA-SPIONs offer highly effective nanoplatforms for drug delivery [128]. The clinical use of magnetic hyperthermia (MH) as an alternate method for the localized treatment of malignancies has begun. When exposed to an alternating magnetic field, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) produce heat that is used by MH [129].

In the field of cancer theranostics, more intense research is currently being conducted using 2D nanographene oxide (GO) and zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Kumawat and colleagues developed a graphene oxide-polyethylene imine-graphene quantum dots hybrid (GO-PEI-GQDs). Regarding imaging, photothermal, and oxidative stress responses in breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) the quantum dot hybrid acted as a functional nanomaterial. GO-PEI-GQDs showed a great photothermal response (44–49 °C) when subjected to an 808 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min at a concentration of up to 50 g/mL [130]. Iqbal et al. suggested a novel liquid-phase approach for generating multimodal bidirectional metal oxide-based nanomaterials known as Janus nanoparticles (JNPs), which are based on Mn3O4–TiO2/ZnO/Fe3O4. Pluronic® F-127-coated Mn3O4–TiO2 JNPs were employed in vitro and in vivo with T1-W MRI and PDT for malignancies. T1-W MRI of the heart, liver, and kidneys in mice after intravenous administration of the nanoparticles revealed their excellent sensitivity and biocompatibility as Mn3O4–TiO2 JNPs. Compared to the control, the viability of cells incubated with Mn3O4–TiO2 nanocomposites was 50% lower under mild UV exposure (without UV irradiation). Inorganic TiO2 PSs have exhibited excellent function in lowering efficacy in PDT in vitro and in vivo at low UV irradiation (5.6 mW/cm2) because of their nanosized size and photodegradable stability. These findings reveal that multifunctional Mn3O4–TiO2 JNPs improve T1-w MRI contrast while also having excellent PDT capabilities, implying that they could be used as cancer theranostics agents [131]. Additionally, the Mn3O4–TiO2@PF-127 probe effectively eliminated tumors in vivo under low intensity UV light at the administered dose with no discernible side effects. Finally, this study demonstrates the significant theranostic potential for the development of multifunctional probes for imaging-guided cancer therapy as well as binary transition metal-oxide-based JNP production.

In cancer theranostics, transformable liquid metal (LM)-based materials have captivated researchers' curiosity [132]. However, the potential biomedical applications of LMs have yet to be substantially investigated [133]. Wang et al. investigated the inductive heating properties of gallium-indium eutectic alloy (EGaln) under different magnetic fields. Compared to ordinary magnetite nanoparticles, LM possesses exceptional magnetic healing qualities and can be employed in magnetic hyperthermia as a nonmagnetic substance. Additionally, a simple PEGylation approach could greatly decrease the high surface tension of LM, making it an excellent vehicle for various theranostic products. By incorporating DOX-loaded mesoporous silica (DOX-MS) into PEGylated LM, a magnetic field-driven reconfigurable LM hybrid model suited for the pH/AFM dual stimuli-responsive release of drug and magnetic thermo-chemotherapy was successfully established [134]. It has been shown how it might be used to treat breast cancer. Additionally, the hybrid has a promising ability for CT imaging owing to the significant X-ray attenuation ability of LM. A prospective cancer treatment protocol based on an LM hybrid for magnetic hyperthermia in conjunction with dual stimuli-responsive chemotherapy and CT imaging has been investigated. To enhance PDT and MRI efficacy, Hao et al. synthesized biocompatible and highly potential polylactic-coglycolic acid (PLGA) NPs using hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether and MnO2 as T1 agents and drug carriers [135].

Nano theranostics is a rapidly developing medicinal technique for concurrently detecting drug delivery and transport, as well as evaluating real-time therapeutic efficacy through a single nanoscale for the treatment and diagnosis of fatal diseases such as cancers [40]. Chemically produced nanoparticles have a few drawbacks in terms of cost, toxicity, and efficacy [136]. Because of their low cost, ease of production, and low toxicity, bioinspired organic nanomaterials have maintained an advantage over conventionally manufactured nanomaterials in this context. Organic nanoparticles that resemble natural body components have recently received much attention due to their broad applicability as biocompatible drug carriers in cancer theranostics [137]. The most significant benefit of these nanomaterials is the fundamental changes in the systemic distribution of drugs that ordinary drug delivery techniques lack. The following section of this review will attempt to provide a comprehensive overview of many different types of organic nanomaterials or biomaterials including biodegradable polymers, liposomes, bio-synthesized metal nanoparticles, amino acids, peptides, polysaccharides, and so on used in cancer theranostics that have been explored in recent years.

Biodegradable polymers have indeed been identified as key carriers for the delivery of drugs and imaging agents in the last two decades, assisting in the development of multimodal formulation [138]. Yi et al. generated multifunctional nanoparticles based on ultra-small hyperbranched semiconducting polymer (HSP) nanoparticles for tagging radioactive elements such as technetium-99 m (99mTc), iodine-131 (131I), and iodine-125 (125I). In two independent cancerous tumors, intramuscular xenografts, and patient-derived xenografts, SPECT imaging of 99mTc tagged PEGylated HSP nanoparticles (HSP-PEG) demonstrated considerable accumulation. As a tumor-vascular disrupting agent (VDA), 5,6-dimethylxanthenone-4-acetic acid (DMXAA) greatly increases the tumor accumulation of 131I-tagged HSP-PEG nanoparticles, resulting in improved tumor growth suppression after intravenous infusion [139]. Pan and colleagues developed a Pluronic F127 (PF127) copolymer with an inner core containing the heptamethine cyanine dye me-IR825. At 610 nm and 845 nm stimulated by 550 nm, the PF127/me-IR825 NPs were excited by 780 nm. Early diagnosis of cancer/normal cell differentiation and cancer was achieved previously with in vitro mitochondrial fluorescence imaging. In vivo, NIR fluorescence imaging has also been performed. The NPs could also be employed for in vivo photoacoustic imaging at an excitation wavelength of 808 nm. The NPs showed good photothermal tumor eradication in vitro and in vivo, and the PF127/me-IR825 NPs will be used as theranostic nanosystems for cancer therapy [140].

Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs), unlike traditional liposomes, have a micelle-like structure with the drug encapsulated in a hydrophobic center [141]. Granja et al. constructed mitoxantrone-loaded SLNs, which demonstrated decreased toxicity and increased bioavailability. A low-cost, organic-solvent-free process was used to produce the SLN, and it was further improved utilizing a Box-Behnken design. Even in situations with high lipid contents, nanoparticles were hemo and cytocompatible. The promise of the nanosystem for tumor drug administration was demonstrated by the significantly higher drug release at acidic pH (mimicking the endosomal compartment) than plasmatic pH. Finally, heterotypic breast cancer spheroids used in the evaluation of the nanoformulation demonstrated their potential to penetrate the tridimensional structure and to provide a potent antitumor impact that was comparable to that of the free medication [142]. Fig. 6 illustrates that SELS is a synergistic targeted liposome nanocarrier system in which a cancer identifying ligand (anti-oestrogen receptor antibody) and an immune-targeting ligand (self-peptide (SP)) are covalently bound. Wang et al. reported that anti-oestrogen receptor antibodies could directly bind to oestrogen receptor-positive breast cancer tissues. SP stimulated the CD47-SIRP immunological response, which inhibited nanoparticle uptake by macrophages.

In live systems, the enhanced targeting capacity and anti-phagocytosis activity of nanocarriers may boost tumor absorption and prevent immune clearance. The transportation efficiency and bioavailability of nanoparticles were assessed using NIR scanning on the Maestro in vivo spectrum imaging system in nude mice with MCF-7 xenograft cancers. Mice were treated with 1 mg/mL Dir SELSDir, ELSDir, SLSDir, and LSDir in 200 mL PBS containing DMSO through the caudal vein. The control group was PBS. The buildup of SELSDir in MCF-7 cancer cells was monitored over time. In the targeted (SELSDir) group, a substantial fluorescence signal developed in cancer and culminated at 10 h. As a result of the drug-loaded SELS, improved tumor imaging and therapy efficacy in live systems were achieved [143]. In addition to anticancer medications and gene carriers, ultrasound microbubbles are commonly used in scientific investigations [144]. Chen et al. synthesized MOF coupled with HMONs and a polydopamine (PDA) layer to construct new molecular organic/inorganic hybridized nanocomposites (HMON-PMOF) as shown in Fig. 7 . DOX and indocyanine green (ICG) were loaded with great loading efficacy into the inside chamber of HMONs and the outer porous shell of the MOF, respectively. The dual drug-loaded nanomaterials (DI@HMON-PMOF) produced had good photothermal properties and a pH/NIR-triggered DOX release pattern. In vitro cell tests also showed that HMONs-PMOF could effectively transport DOX to cancer cells [145].

To solve the challenges of pancreatic tumor treatment, biodegradable nanoplatforms based on HMONs were developed by Gao et al. The team designed a biodegradable nanostructure and improved antitumor efficacy by lowering ECM levels and delivering pirfenidone (PFD) to the targeted site [146]. Due to their biologically active disulfide bond architecture and substantial surface area, HMON nanoparticles with tumor-sensitive biodegradability were chosen as the best drug carriers. By reacting with an aldehyde functional group on the surface, Gem, a tiny molecular anticancer medication containing functional hydroxide groups, was further integrated onto the pore channel of PFD-loaded HMON nanoparticles. In this design, the gem served as both the "gatekeeper" and the chemotherapeutic. The resulting acetal bond on the PFD@HMON-Gem complex, which acts as a pH-sensitive gatekeeper, can be sufficiently broken in the mildly acidic tumor ECM microenvironment. The expression of certain ECM components will be reduced as a result of effective PFD therapy, and this will eventually increase the accessibility of nanomedicines into the underlying tumor tissue.

Zhao et al. introduced a multi-ultrasound molecular probe as a cancer theranostic material. The molecular probes are called phase-transformation lipid nanoparticles, which consist of hyaluronic acid-mediated cell-penetrating peptide-modified 10-hydroxycamptothecin-loaded (HA/CPPs-10-HCPT-NPs). These probes have been combined with low-intensity focused ultrasound (LIFU) for extremely accurate hepatocellular carcinoma treatment interventions (HCC). As a naturally occurring macromolecular polysaccharide with excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, HA has anti-immune effects. HA has a strong affinity for some overexpressed markers, such as CD44, seen on tumor cell surfaces. Additionally, HA can improve receptor-mediated endocytosis when combined with medicines, enabling the treatment to actively target focused areas without harming normal cells and enhancing its therapeutic index. The acidic extracellular matrix of tumor cells contains a large amount of hyaluronidase (HAase), which can hydrolyze HA to reveal CPPs in HA-coated NPs. NPs that have been altered with particular compounds have proven to be excellent carriers of chemotherapeutic drugs as well as potential drivers for targeting cells and tissues. In terms of anti-proliferation and apoptosis rates, the HA/CPPs-10-HCPT-NPs + LIFU synthesized group outperformed most other groups. In mouse liver tumor xenografts, HA/CPPs-10-HCPT-NPs adequately achieved tumor sites and increased ultrasound imaging. In comparison to the other groups, the HA/CPPs-10-HCPT-NPs + LIFU group had smaller tumors, a lower proliferative index (PI), and relatively strong tumorigenesis and apoptotic index [147] (Fig. 8 ).

Li et al. synthesized F8-PEG NPs, a biodegradable PTT medication for exceptionally efficient cancer theranostics. The nanoparticles achieved a photothermal efficiency of up to 82% using an oligomer with outstanding NIR absorption, outperforming previously reported physicochemical and biological PTT agents. Oligomer nanoparticles are also photostable and biodegradable. The biosafety and PTT effectiveness of F8-PEG NPs were investigated in vitro and in vivo. This study describes an oligomer-based PTT agent that appears to be promising and sheds light on the development of highly effective nanomaterials for cancer theranostics [148]. Fig. 9 shows a 4T1 tumor xenograft model with the photothermal efficacy of F8-PEG NPs. At various time intervals, IR thermal photographs of mice treated with four formulations (PBS, F8-PEG NPs, laser, or F8-PEG NPs + laser, laser: 808 nm, 1 W/cm2) were obtained. Following the self-assembly of F8-IC with PEG, the F8-PEG NPs display a high PCE of 82% for high-performance photoacoustic imaging-guided cancer therapy. To our knowledge, this PTT substance has the greatest PCE of all those that have been documented. Particularly, after performing their photothermic function, F8-PEG NPs could be biodegraded by endogenous enzymes to satisfy concerns regarding long-term toxicity. In addition to having good size- and photostability, the F8-PEG NPs also have exceptional therapeutic efficacy both in vitro and in vivo. Together, our findings show that oligomeric nanoparticles might be the best type of PTT agent and offer guidance for developing organic nanoagents with exceptional potency for cancer theranostics.

However, premature renal excretion limits its biomedical applicability due to the small size of these particles. Hybrid poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA-CDs) nanomaterials with adequate volume fraction and resilience were created to address these limitations. CDs focus on providing stable PLGA-based nanostructures in an aqueous solution as well as adequate cryoprotection when combined with 1% PVP. However, PLGA nanoparticles required at least 5% sucrose for increased bioavailability (4.5%) and long-term drug release (48 h). FI and MTT cell viability assays were used in vitro on a breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB-231) to confirm therapeutic and imaging perspectives [149]. As a chemotherapeutic agent, asiatic acid (AA) is a potential candidate, but delivering it to patients with glioblastoma is difficult. Garanti et al. examined the therapeutic effects of RGD-conjugated SLNs in glioblastoma for selective AA targeting. When compared to non-RGD-containing SLNs, RGD-SLNs significantly increased AA cytotoxicity against U87-MG monolayer cells and continued to increase cellular absorption. When compared to AA alone or non-RGD-containing SLNs, the efficiency of AA-loaded RGD-SLNs as glioblastoma target-specific therapeutics showed enhanced tumor growth control, constantly improved cytotoxicity, and amplified spheroid insertion in spherical mice [150]. Mansur et al. used a simple green aqueous process to develop a bi-functional quantum dots/polysaccharide/folate nanoconjugate (Cu–In–S and Cu–In–S/ZnS nanoparticles treated with carboxymethylcellulose biopolymer). They proved nano theranostics characteristics for potential drug delivery and excellent imaging characteristics and vectorization that were efficient in vitro at killing breast cancer cells. Using the MTT assay in vitro, the cell viability results suggested that the nanoconjugates were nontoxic. These hybrids provide new nanoplatforms for cancer theranostics applications by utilizing fluorescent polysaccharide-drug conjugates [151].

Natural polysaccharides can easily produce biodegradable nanogels, and their elasticity and tenderness may allow them to penetrate human skin more easily yet retain therapeutic bioactivity in comparison to stiff nanoparticles [152]. Nanogels offer strong biocompatibility, relatively high dispersibility, and a well-defined compact structure when compared to other nanocarriers [153]. Pei et al. employed crosslinking of folate-terminated polyethylene glycol (FA-PEG-NH2) and rhodamine B (RhB)-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) to create an alginate-based cancer-associated, stimuli-driven effect, and transform therapeutic and diagnostic drug carrier nanogels for cancer theranostics, such as, chemotherapy (RhB-PEG-NH2) The FR-mediated targeting and pH/reduction bidirectional responsive intracellular triggered ejection of DOX was achieved primarily due to the FA groups, covalent bridging complexes, and Schiff base conjugation of DOX [154]. Du et al. synthesized spirulina polysaccharides into spirulina polysaccharide-loaded nanoemulsions (SPS-NEs) and investigated their antitumor activity when combined with paclitaxel (PTX). The anticancer efficacy of SPS-NEs in combination with PTX was tested using S180 cells or RAW 264.7 macrophages, and S180 tumor-bearing rats. SPS-NEs can enhance PTX's antitumor activity by modulating immunity via TLR4/NF–B signaling pathways [155]. The objective of this study was to prepare SPS-NEs, increase the stability of SPS, examine the anti-S180 tumor effects of PTX + SPS-NEs, and determine if SPS-NEs could increase the immunostimulatory effect of PTX activity in the tumor microenvironment via the TLR4/NF–B pathway.

Protein-based nanoplatforms have incited the most problems due to their biodegradability, biocompatibility, lack of or low toxicity, and ease of modification. Protein-based therapeutic and diagnostic platforms can be further designed with functional molecules for various purposes in the presence of reactive groups such as –COOH, –NH2, and –OH. Han et al. conjugated gemcitabine (GEM) to human serum albumin (HSA) via the cathepsin B cleavable peptide GFLG and subsequently complexed it with the NIR dye IR780 which resulted in unique enzyme-sensitive albumin-based GEM delivery nanoplatforms. BxPC-3 pancreatic tumor xenografted mice were used to test the in vivo performance of the HSA-GEM/IR780 complex. In vivo NIR imaging demonstrated that HSA-GEM/IR780 had greater uptake and long-term retention in tumor tissues than free IR780. Meanwhile, conditions that facilitate GEM nano vectors into inactive 2′,2′-difluorodeoxyuridine (dFdU) can be considerably reduced compared to free GEM. The activated version of GEM (gemcitabine triphosphate, dFdCTP) was shown to have a substantially higher concentration in tumor tissue, indicating improved tumor suppression effectiveness with few adverse effects [156]. An et al. successfully developed fluorescent protein nanoparticles by combining tiny ultrared fluorescent protein nanoparticles (smURFP) with bovine serum albumin. The quantum yield of these fluorescent nanoparticles is 17%, and their diameter is 12–14 nm. Infrared fluorescent protein nanoparticles can image tumors in living mice via an enhanced permeability and retention mechanism [157]. Benign and malignant stage cancers can be targeted using theranostics and dual-modality imaging with positron emission tomography for complete human imaging utilizing fluorescent protein nanoparticles that act as smart nanomaterials.

Combination chemotherapy is becoming increasingly used in the development of cancer therapy [158]. First, combined chemotherapy can effectively prevent multidrug resistance. Second, in a synergistic or combination approach, several anticancer medicines with diverse action targets may boost treatment success. Nanocarrier application in vitro and tumor accumulation in vivo can be greatly improved by ligand-based active targeting or EPR-based passive targeting. Finally, medications carried in nanocarriers can avoid early breakdown in the circulatory system [159–161]. Wang et al. designed a DOX and cisplatin synergetic targeted combination treatment for breast cancer using HER2 antibody-decorated nanoparticles composed of aldehyde hyaluronic acid (AHA) and hydroxyethyl chitosan (HECS). DOX and cisplatin were gradually linked onto AHA using chelation and Schiff's base reaction, resulting in a DOX/cisplatin-loaded AHA inner core. The core was sequentially crosslinked with HECS to target HER2-antibody-conjugated AHA. On the created near-spherical nanoplatforms, which had an average size of 160 nm and a zeta potential of 28 mV, pH-responsive surface charge flipping, and release of drugs were reported. The active targeting of the HER2 receptor significantly improved the nanoplatform cellular uptake [162]. Wang et al. allegedly synthesized multimodal polysaccharide-based nanostructures from aldehyde hyaluronic acid (AHA) and hydroxyethyl chitosan (HECS) via layer-by-layer self-assembly, achieving combination chemotherapy and photodynamic treatment [163].

Because of their unique properties, such as desired targeting, easy loading of anticancer medicinal products, pH-responsive drug dissolution, and surface charge reversion, nanoparticles made of negatively charged polyelectrolytes have attracted considerable attention [164]. These polyelectrolyte-based nanoparticles can also be coated with biomolecules such as antibodies, particular peptides, folic acid, and other ligands to accomplish active targeting via their unique interactions with tumor cell receptors [165,166]. To control drug release for metastatic cancer, Chai et al. used layer-by-layer self-assembly to seed organic polyelectrolyte layers of biomaterials such as chitosan (CHI) and alginate (ALG) on DOX-loaded PLGA NPs. Chitosan (CHI) and alginate (ALG) natural polyelectrolyte multilayers were alternately layered on poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles (NPs) loaded with DOX to regulate drug release for anticancer activity. The effects of temperature, polyelectrolyte concentration, and NaCl concentration on the multilayer development of nano-colloidal particles were investigated and the CHI/ALG multilayers' development phase was then confirmed. In vitro studies demonstrated that DOX-loaded PLGA NPs (DOX-PLGA NPs) exhibited an unwanted immediate discharge, which was minimized utilizing the layer-by-layer technique from 55.12% to 5.78%. When the pH of the solution was reduced from 7.4 to 5.0, the release of DOX increased by more than 40%. When compared to DOX-PLGA NPs and DOX in solution, DOX-PLGA (CHI/ALG)3 NPs demonstrated greater in vitro potential therapeutic rates of 83.17% and lower toxicity. As a result, PLGA-polyelectrolyte NPs offer a large future in terms of regulated anticancer medication release in nanotechnology [167].

To improve nanocarrier transportation into the tumor and metastatic regions and improve TME reactive drug release, stimulus-sensitive nanocarriers have been induced in response to physiological stimuli such as low pH and redox potentials [168]. Cysteamine was used to construct redox/enzyme-sensitive chondroitin sulfate-ss-deoxycholic acid (CSCD) conjugates that could self-assemble in aquatic media, resulting in self-assembled nanoparticles (175.6 + 5.2 nm). Docetaxel (DTX) was loaded in nanoparticles with the needed loading efficiency to reduce melanoma tumor development and metastasis, according to Liu et al. The enzyme hyaluronidase-1 (Hyal-1) was discovered to react with nanoparticles, breaking down the backbones of chondroitin sulfate (CS). In the presence of GSH/Hyal-1, scientists created dual-sensitive nanoparticles with an improved release of the drug pathway. When compared to Taxotere®, CSCD nanoparticles significantly improved DTX circulation in tumors and lungs, with a 4.4-fold higher area-under-the-curve (AUC) value. DTX-loaded CSCD nanoparticles decreased in vitro tumor size and lung metastatic growth by inducing apoptosis and lowering metastasis-promoting protein expression [169] (Fig. 10 ). The synergistic effects of Hyal-1-induced CS degradation and GSH-induced disulfide bond cleavage in CSCD nanoparticles should not only increase the effectiveness of active targeted drug delivery in tumors but also facilitate regulated release. These findings strongly supported the dissociation of CSCD nanoparticles in response to Hyal-1, and they also suggested a possible use of DTX administration for the prevention of tumor growth and metastasis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of nanomaterials in cancer theranostics

ce:section.ce:para: Laser and radiation therapy are often used to treat non-metastatic cancers, whereas anticancer medications such as biological therapy, hormone therapy, and chemotherapy are commonly used to successfully treat last-stage cancers. Antibiotics, ligands, therapies, proteins, and peptides are all transported by NPs, which target tissues with minimal toxicity and long-term drug delivery. As a result, tumor-targeting is a critical benefit of nanotechnology in cancer treatment. Nanomaterials and nanocompo-sites can be designed or carefully manufactured to discriminate cancerous tissue from normal cells and preferentially eliminate cancerous tissue with little interference from healthy cells to achieve this objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of the tumor microenvironment (TME) with nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The condition of the tumor microenvironment (TME) is essential for the initiation of cancer. TME composition also has a substantial influence on the effectiveness of cancer treatment as well as the prognosis of cancer. Because the TME has been shown to modify the functioning of aberrant tissues and contribute to the formation of cell malignancies, researchers are seeking to discover and develop novel cancer therapeutic interventions that successfully target the TME. TME-targeted procedures often involve a variety of targeting regimens, allowing for the integration of nanosystems. Nanomaterials appear to be a potential method for controlling the TME. Considering the fundamental physiological traits of all tumor cells, certain TME-responsive NMs are being examined with a focus on TME-associated pH abnormalities, hypoxia, enzymes, redox environment, and ROS to develop a global cancer treatment strategy.

NMs are extremely helpful in imaging applications because of the large surface area to volume ratio and the ability of chemical changes to improve image sensitivity. This approach can provide a wide range of pharmacological and diagnostic chemicals. Only a few of the many advantages include improved diagnostics, tumor-specific pharmaceutical administration, and fewer lethal effects on normal tissues. Targeted medicine delivery is combined with diagnostic imaging modalities including optical imaging, CT scan, MRI, and ultrasonic imaging in these theranostic treatments. Furthermore, by employing specialized probes, selecting the relevant imaging techniques and compounds, and combining them in the right way for different therapeutic reasons, all these imaging modalities can demonstrate pharmacological efficacy throughout the drug development phases.

Traditional pharmaceutical formulations have several drawbacks that nanotechnology can help overcome. Engineered nanomaterials have made tremendous advances in cancer treatment by improving sensitivity, specificity, and efficacy. Engineered nanoparticles that have been functionalized with certain ligands can reliably deliver encapsulated carriers to cancer cells. The structure, shape, size, and surface properties of nanomaterials can be altered to improve drug absorption and, cell half-life, regulate discharge, and provide excellent distribution. Because of their nanoscale size and unique physicochemical properties, polymeric nanoparticles, metallic nanoparticles, carbon-based materials, liposomes, and protein-based nanomaterials have all been developed as smart drug delivery systems for cancer treatment, with better involvement in the generation of pharmacokinetic and potential drug delivery characteristics than conventional therapies. All of these methods can limit systemic toxicity at tumor sites by ensuring that healthy cells are not harmed. Several nanoplatforms have also been engineered to release drug carriers in response to a variety of stimuli, allowing for multifunctionality and specificity. Despite the advantages of nanomaterials in cancer theranostics, clinical adaptation is still a challenge. Medical studies are failing due to a lack of understanding of the harmful consequences of nanoformulations and their in vivo behaviors. As a result, just a few nanomedicines for cancer treatment are now available on the market. However, further advancements in nanomedicine will signal a fundamental change in the treatment of cancer and drastically improve clinical care [170]. Theranostic nanoparticles now in the industry include all three components—an imaging agent, a therapeutic agent, and a targeting substituent in their primary design concept, as previously discussed in the sections above. For the development of appropriate theranostic applications, all three components require in-depth examination. Theranostic nanomaterials must be constructed taking into consideration the photothermal and photodermal properties for the generation of ideal chemotherapeutic agents to appropriately fulfill the therapeutic demand. By incorporating these qualities, one can anticipate the development of effective anticancer treatments, or tailored cancer therapies, that can address the many variants of cancer in all patients. Yet again, this goal demands in-depth knowledge of both the material needs for contemporary clinical imaging modalities and the mechanisms of cancer growth. Fig. 11 illustrates various nanomaterial-based structure types, imaging systems, targeted drug deliveries, and cancer therapeutics.

According to World Health Organization (WHO) statistics, cancer has killed approximately 9.6 million people worldwide annually since 2018. Around 70% of cancer fatalities occur in countries where economic levels are high. Currently, poverty is also high, and employment is low, due to the pandemic thus many cannot afford the diagnosis and treatment of early-stage cancer. As previously stated, traditional clinical diagnosis approaches are unsuccessful in the early stages of cancer. Therapeutic and diagnostic treatments are being developed to assist patients in extending their overall survival time due to the physiological and clinical difficulties linked to cancer growth and development. It is necessary to target cancer tissues to improve nanocarrier transportation into the tumor and metastatic regions and improve TME reactive drug release. Therefore, the discovery of smart nanomaterials has provided new possible approaches for effective cancer therapy. Chemotherapy, phototherapy, gene therapy, combination therapy, and theranostics are among the inorganic and organic nanomaterials studied in vitro and in vivo for cancer treatment. Polymeric nanomaterials, metallic nanoparticles, carbon-based materials, liposomes, and protein-based nanomaterials or other materials have been researched extensively in cancer theranostics. Smart nanomaterials have been built employing a variety of fabrication and preparation processes and material selections to overcome the constraints of one-component systems. Low-cost and easily produced nanomaterials with high cellular absorption, lengthy circulation durations, and site-specificity are being studied for cancer imaging and diagnosis, as well as therapeutic agents. Furthermore, nanoparticles with a high surface-to-volume ratio can transport a range of drugs to the target site and retain them. As a result, smart nanomaterial-basedcancer therapies are more effective while having fewer side effects. As technology develops, nanomaterials for cancer theranostics will move from the research laboratory to the clinic. Table 2 shows the comparison of some representative nanomaterials, physiological properties, targeting sites and usage associated with cancer theranostic applications. In conclusion, future research into safe, efficient, and smart nanomaterials (SMs) will lead to significant advancements in cancer diagnosis and therapy, particularly in terms of a better understanding of cancer biology and the advancement of science and technology.
